skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Comprehensive spreadsheet creation, editing, and analysis with support for formulas, formatting, data analysis, and visualization. When Claude needs to work with spreadsheets (.xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, .tsv, etc) for: (1) Creating new spreadsheets with formulas and formatting, (2) Reading or analyzing data, (3) Modify existing spreadsheets while preserving formulas, (4) Data analysis and visualization in spreadsheets, or (5) Recalculating formulas
skill.summary: Comprehensive spreadsheet creation, editing, and analysis tool for Excel files with formula support
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 59 static findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate spreadsheet operations tool using openpyxl for Excel file manipulation and LibreOffice for formula recalculation. Network URLs are legal documentation links. External commands invoke soffice (LibreOffice) - a standard spreadsheet application. Filesystem access is limited to user config directories for macro setup. Code examples in SKILL.md use Markdown formatting. Excel formulas flagged as C2 keywords are standard spreadsheet syntax.

content.value_statement: This skill enables Claude to create, edit, and analyze Excel spreadsheets with formulas, formatting, and data analysis. It solves the problem of manual spreadsheet work by automating file creation and modification tasks.
actual_capabilities: Create new Excel files with formulas, formatting, and styling using openpyxl
Read and analyze data from existing Excel files using pandas and openpyxl
Modify existing spreadsheets while preserving formulas and formatting
Recalculate formulas in Excel files using LibreOffice
Apply financial model color coding standards (blue for inputs, black for formulas)
Detect and report Excel formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, etc.)
limitations: Requires LibreOffice installation for formula recalculation feature
Does not support password-protected Excel files
Cannot execute macros or VBA code within Excel files
Large files may require increased timeout settings

use_cases.title: Financial Analyst Creates Budget Model
use_cases.description: A financial analyst needs to build a quarterly budget model with color-coded inputs and formulas that auto-calculate totals and variances.

use_cases.title: Data Analyst Merges Data Sources
use_cases.description: A data analyst wants to read multiple CSV files, combine the data, and export to a formatted Excel report with summary calculations.
use_cases.target_user: Data analysts and business intelligence professionals

use_cases.title: Project Manager Tracks Deliverables
use_cases.description: A project manager needs to create a tracking spreadsheet with status columns, conditional formatting, and automated progress calculations.

prompt_templates.title: Create Simple Spreadsheet
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel file named [filename.xlsx] with the following data: [describe data]. Include formulas for [describe calculations].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Existing Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the Excel file [filename.xlsx] and provide a summary of the data including: total rows, column names, any missing values, and basic statistics.

prompt_templates.title: Update Financial Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the Excel file [filename.xlsx] by: 1) Changing the growth rate in cell [cell] to [value], 2) Adding a new column for [description], 3) Ensuring all formulas recalculate correctly.
prompt_templates.scenario: Modifying existing spreadsheet with preserved formulas

prompt_templates.title: Build Complex Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a professional Excel report with: multiple sheets (Summary, Details, Charts), pivot-style summaries, conditional formatting for [conditions], and proper number formatting for currency/percentages.

output_examples.input: Create a sales report with monthly revenue in column B, YoY growth in column C using formulas, and green text for positive growth, red for negative.
output_examples.output: Created sales_report.xlsx with 3 sheets (Summary, Monthly Data, YoY Analysis). Applied formula =((B3-B2)/B2) for growth calculation. Green font (RGB 0,128,0) for positive values, red (RGB 255,0,0) for negative. All formulas verified with zero errors.

output_examples.input: Read the budget.xlsx file and tell me which categories have variances greater than 10%.
output_examples.output: Analyzed budget.xlsx (5 sheets, 1,240 rows). Found 3 categories with variances over 10%: Marketing (-15.2%), Travel (-12.8%), Software (+11.4%). Summary table created with variance percentages and status indicators.

best_practices: Always use Excel formulas instead of hardcoded calculated values to maintain spreadsheet interactivity
Apply proper number formatting (currency symbols, decimal places, negative number display) for readability
Test formulas on sample cells before applying across large datasets
Use the recalc.py script after any modification to verify formula integrity
anti_patterns: Do not calculate values in Python and hardcode them into cells - use Excel formulas instead
Avoid opening files with data_only=True and saving, as this permanently deletes formulas
Do not ignore formula errors - always run recalc.py and fix #REF!, #DIV/0!, and other errors
Avoid verbose variable names and excessive comments in generated Python code

faq.question: Does this skill work with .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, and .tsv files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports .xlsx (Excel with formulas), .xlsm (macro-enabled), .csv (comma-separated), and .tsv (tab-separated) files.

faq.question: Why do I need to run recalc.py after creating a spreadsheet?
faq.answer: openpyxl saves formulas as text strings without calculating their values. recalc.py uses LibreOffice to actually compute formula results.

faq.question: Can I preserve existing formatting when editing a file?
faq.answer: Yes. Use load_workbook() to open existing files - openpyxl preserves all formatting, formulas, and styles by default.

faq.question: What color coding is used for financial models?
faq.answer: Standard convention: Blue (RGB 0,0,255) for inputs, Black for formulas, Green for worksheet links, Red for external links, Yellow background for key assumptions.

faq.question: How do I handle division by zero errors in formulas?
faq.answer: Use IFERROR or IF with ISNUMBER to check denominators. Example: =IF(B2=0, 0, A2/B2) or =IFERROR(A2/B2, 0).

faq.question: Can this skill read password-protected Excel files?
faq.answer: No. Password-protected files cannot be read or modified by openpyxl due to security restrictions.
